chain_of_thought: Tools are unreachable (connection refused), so I must reason from metadata only.

Crash is strchr_default inside decodeBytes -> Java_...jniParse with fuzz harness entry. strchr_default suggests libc strchr being called with invalid pointer or unterminated data, likely from decodeBytes processing the input byte array.

jniParse([B)J directly takes a byte[]; fuzz harness probably passes arbitrary bytes without null termination guarantees, while real Java code path shows TBXML.parse(String) -> PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml(String) -> ... -> jniParse([B)J. In production, the byte[] will almost certainly be derived from a well-formed String or file contents, not arbitrary memory.

Still, decodeBytes using strchr on attacker-influenced data without explicit length is a classical unsafe pattern (CWE-676/787). If it assumes null-terminated buffer but only gets a length + raw bytes, fuzz can cause strchr to walk past the buffer and crash or hit unmapped memory.

Because I cannot inspect decodeBytes/JNI, I must infer: name decodeBytes suggests it processes variable-length data; using strchr_default instead of a bounded variant indicates missing length checks, so likely an out-of-bounds read. The crash is not obviously a harness-only artifact since jniParse is reachable from real Java methods taking a String that could originate from external KML content (e.g., network, files).

Thus, this is likely a genuine vulnerability (OOB read / potential DoS), though exploitability to write/RCE is unknown. Severity: medium (reliable crash on malformed KML or String) with moderate confidence.
Affected library is libtbxml.so; app_native_function is decodeBytes; JNI bridge is TBXML.jniParse([B)J. Call sequence: jniParse -> decodeBytes -> strchr_default.
Limitations: can't see exact implementation, can't prove bounds or validation are absent, but typical TBXML decoders known to be unsafe when using C-string APIs on arbitrary byte arrays.
reasons: strchr_default crash inside decodeBytes strongly indicates use of unbounded C string search on attacker-influenced buffer
jniParse([B)J is reachable from Java API that can consume external KML/String data, so malformed input can trigger this path in production
Lack of evidence of length checks or null-termination guarantees before calling strchr implies potential out-of-bounds read / denial-of-service

evidence.note: Top application-level native frame below strchr_default; likely passes attacker-influenced buffer to strchr without length bounding.
JNI entry taking byte[] from Java and bridging into decodeBytes; no indication of native-side validation.
Java-visible JNI bridge reachable from PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml(String), which can be fed arbitrary KML strings.

recommendations: Inspect decodeBytes implementation and replace raw strchr usage with a bounded variant (e.g., memchr) using the actual buffer length
Ensure JNI layer passes both pointer and explicit length to decoding functions and enforces that input is properly null-terminated or length-checked
Add robust input validation and fuzz testing around TBXML.jniParse to confirm no unbounded string operations remain
assumptions: decodeBytes uses strchr or similar unbounded C-string functions directly on the provided buffer
KML/String data consumed by TBXML in real app can be influenced by external or user-provided sources
limitations: Ghidra MCP tools were unavailable, so no direct decompilation of decodeBytes or JNI functions
No direct view of Java implementation of TBXML.jniParse or parse(String) to confirm exact origin and validation of inputs

chain_of_thought: Tools are unavailable (connection refused), so I cannot decompile native functions or inspect Java bodies, but I must still provide an assessment based on the structured crash data and typical patterns.

Native trace shows: strpbrk -> decodeBytes -> Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse. The process termination reason is 'strpbrk', which is a standard C library function that searches a string for any of a set of bytes; typical crashes arise if the input string is not null-terminated or the search set pointer is invalid.

The first app-level frame below libc is decodeBytes in libtbxml.so. Without decompilation I infer this is part of an XML parsing helper (TBXML is a lightweight XML parser). A common pattern is: convert a byte buffer passed from Java into a C string and then parse/search with string functions like strpbrk, strtok, strchr, etc.

The JNI bridge method Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse is called from TBXML.jniParse([B)J, which in turn is reached from TBXML.parse(String) in production plugin code (PluginKmlOverlay->TBXML.parse). In the real app, TBXML.parse will likely convert a Java String KML/XML path or content into a byte array and pass it to native. In the fuzz harness, fuzz_one_input is directly feeding arbitrary bytes into the same JNI bridge, skipping normal constraints such as null-termination or character encoding expectations.

To decide if this is a genuine vulnerability, I must assess whether, under realistic app usage, attacker-controllable data can cause unsafe use of strpbrk. For strpbrk to crash by itself, usually one of its arguments is invalid: either the string pointer does not point to a valid null-terminated region, or the 'accept' set pointer is bad. The 'accept' set is almost always a constant in parsing code (e.g., "></=" etc.), so attacker control is improbable there; the tainted value is the input byte buffer that should represent the string.

If decodeBytes or the JNI code assumes the input [B is already null-terminated or ASCII/UTF-8 without ensuring a terminator within bounds, and then passes that raw pointer to strpbrk, a non-terminated buffer could lead to an out-of-bounds read. However, on Android, Java byte arrays passed through JNI preserve length; a correct native implementation would use the length to either (a) allocate a +1 buffer and append '\0', or (b) avoid C string APIs and instead use length-aware routines. A bug where they omit this and treat arbitrary bytes as a C string is a classic vulnerability pattern (CWE-170 Improper Null Termination / leading to CWE-125 OOB read).

Reachability of attacker input: PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml(String) presumably loads KML data from a URL, file, or app bundle. In a Cordova/Google Maps plugin context, KML overlays often come from remote URLs configured by the app or potentially from user-supplied sources (e.g., server-controlled content). Therefore, the contents of the KML file, not just the path, may influence what TBXML parses. However, the JNI takes a byte array; in the app, this probably holds the XML text as encoded bytes, not arbitrary binary. Despite that, an attacker who can control the XML content can include any byte value except those filtered by the path/content loader. Typically, loading text into a Java String constrains bytes to valid encoding; then converting to bytes via getBytes() on UTF-8 will always be valid sequences and include a terminating null only if explicitly added. So the path from remote input to native is realistic but the input is textual, not fully arbitrary binary like the fuzzer.

The crash arose under fuzzing with arbitrary [B fed into jniParse. It is plausible that the fuzzer passed a byte array not representing valid text and not null-terminated, and the native code failed to guard against such inputs. But would production Java ever pass such a buffer? TBXML.parse(String) likely builds the byte array from a Java String or from file/network I/O routines that ensure valid strings. There is no evidence that any path allows a raw attacker-controlled byte array to be passed directly, bypassing Java string creation. Thus the fuzzer scenario (arbitrary bytes) may be strictly broader than what production allows.

Without decompilation I cannot confirm if decodeBytes uses GetByteArrayElements+GetArrayLength correctly and appends a '\0'. If it does not, then even legitimate text inputs could cause problems in degenerate cases (e.g., extremely large lengths causing OOB read past allocated buffer when scanning with strpbrk). But strpbrk's read will only go out of bounds if there is no null terminator within the allocated region; if decodeBytes allocates exactly 'len+1' and forces buf[len]='\0', no problem. If it wraps the Java array without copying and still calls strpbrk, it's relying on incidental nulls, which is unsafe for arbitrary fuzzed input but might be fine for well-formed UTF-8 text generated by Java's encoding routines (since the underlying array isn't required to have a trailing 0).

Given that: (1) the crash is at a libc string function using fuzzed arbitrary bytes; (2) we lack code proof that production Java or JNI ever violate string/encoding invariants; (3) the normal app path seems to pass a Java String->byte[] rather than arbitrary binary; I lean toward this being a harness/fuzz artifact stressing assumptions (passing malformed, non-textual, non-null-terminated data) rather than a clearly exploitable issue under normal app behavior.

I cannot completely rule out that decodeBytes is buggy and would mis-handle even well-formed input (e.g., not allocating space for '\0' and then strpbrk overruns by at most 1 byte), but with no code evidence this remains speculative. The safer classification is 'not confirmed vulnerability'; potential risk is an out-of-bounds read (likely DoS, maybe info leak) if bad XML or extremely long strings are processed, but current data does not establish a realistic attacker-controlled trigger.
Therefore I mark is_vulnerable=false with moderate-low confidence, citing fuzz-only conditions and missing MCP evidence. Severity is left null because no confirmed exploitable pattern is established.
reasons: Crash occurs in libc strpbrk called by decodeBytes under arbitrary fuzzed input, suggesting misuse of C string APIs but only under non-realistic conditions
Java call graph indicates normal usage passes a String-derived byte array, not arbitrary bytes, so real-world inputs are likely constrained to well-formed text
Lack of decompiled native code or validation logic prevents confirming out-of-bounds read or other memory corruption reachable with realistic attacker-controlled data
recommendations: Manually inspect libtbxml.so decodeBytes and JNI jniParse implementations to ensure byte arrays are converted to properly null-terminated buffers before using C string APIs
Add defensive checks in JNI to reject non-textual or unexpected binary byte arrays and enforce length limits before native parsing
Re-run fuzzing with source-level instrumentation on libtbxml to determine exact conditions causing strpbrk failure and whether they are reachable from production Java paths
assumptions: TBXML.parse(String) constructs the byte[] argument for jniParse only from Java Strings or well-formed text sources
decodeBytes uses standard JNI patterns (GetByteArrayElements/GetArrayLength) but its exact handling of null-termination is unknown
Fuzzer bypasses normal application-level preconditions and may provide inputs that cannot occur through real app UI or network flows
limitations: Ghidra MCP backend was unreachable, so no decompilation of decodeBytes or JNI function was possible
Jadx MCP access to TBXML.parse and jniParse implementations was not available, so exact argument construction and validation remain unknown
No concrete input sample or crash log with register/memory state was provided to precisely characterize the fault mode in strpbrk
